QF.id: Concept and Role of Mutual Fund_21

question.text: A mutual fund scheme has total assets worth Rs. 500 crore and total liabilities (including accrued expenses) of Rs. 5 crore. The scheme has 49.50 crore units outstanding. What is the NAV per unit of this scheme?

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** NAV (Net Asset Value) is calculated using the formula: NAV = (Total Assets - Total Liabilities) / Total Number of Outstanding Units
NAV = (Rs. 500 crore - Rs. 5 crore) / 49.50 crore units NAV = Rs. 495 crore / 49.50 crore units NAV = Rs. 10.00 per unit
The NAV represents the per-unit market value of the scheme after deducting all liabilities and expenses.
**The Trap:** Option B (Rs. 10.10) is what you get if you forget to subtract liabilities: 500/49.50 = 10.10. This is the most common error. Option C (Rs. 100) could result from a decimal place error. Option D (Rs. 9.90) might be picked by candidates who incorrectly add liabilities instead of subtracting. Always remember: NAV = (Assets MINUS Liabilities) / Units.
**Cross-Exam Context:** NAV calculation is a fundamental formula tested across NISM V-A, V-B, and XII. In NISM V-B, you also need to know that NAV must be calculated at the end of every business day and published by 11 PM. AMFI website displays NAV of all schemes. NAV is calculated up to 4 decimal places.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Help clients understand that NAV is like a daily "price tag" for each unit. When markets go up, assets increase, so NAV rises. When markets fall, NAV declines. The expense ratio is already deducted before arriving at NAV, so the return clients see is after costs. Never compare NAVs across different funds -- a fund with NAV Rs. 500 is not "costlier" than one with NAV Rs. 20.
